coverage_limits: Assessment is limited to scenario.md and artifacts/facts.json.
Optimality is evaluated only for exact sequential comparison sorting over arbitrary keys with no preprocessing.

rigor.trigger: A single asymptotic optimality claim requires a matching upper bound and comparison-model lower bound.
rigor.tier_reason: The claim is resolved by a direct decision-tree counting argument using the supplied frozen model.

decision_frame.question: Is an O(n log n) comparison sort asymptotically optimal under the supplied model?
decision_frame.system_boundary: Exact sequential comparison sorting over arbitrary keys without preprocessing.
alternatives: O(n log n) exact comparison sorting
hypothetical o(n log n) exact comparison sorting
authorized_objectives: determine the asymptotically optimal comparison complexity
decision_frame.priority_rule: Combine a valid lower bound for every in-model algorithm with the supplied upper bound.
assumptions: Complexity is measured by the number of sequential key comparisons.
The lower bound may be proved on the subset of inputs having distinct keys.
empirical_premises: artifacts/facts.json states that the model is sequential comparison, exact, without preprocessing, over arbitrary keys, and has an O(n log n) algorithm.
decision_frame.uncertainty_posture: Do not extend the conclusion beyond the frozen model or infer unstated thresholds or classes.

derivations.construct: information-theoretic lower bound matched to a supplied upper bound
derivations.model: Exact sequential comparison sorting over arbitrary keys, with no preprocessing.
preconditions: The algorithm must correctly sort every permitted input.
Consider n distinct keys, which are included among arbitrary-key inputs.
A comparison between distinct keys has two possible outcomes.

fact_mapping.exact: Each distinct input ordering must lead to its correct output ordering.
fact_mapping.sequential_comparison: An execution is represented by a binary comparison decision tree.
fact_mapping.arbitrary_keys: The algorithm must handle the n! permutations of n distinct keys.
fact_mapping.upper_bound: artifacts/facts.json supplies an O(n log n) algorithm in the model.
fact_mapping.preprocessing: No preprocessing is available outside the counted model.

steps: Restrict attention to inputs consisting of n distinct keys; a lower bound on this subset is a lower bound for the full input domain.
There are n! possible input permutations, and exact sorting must distinguish them, so the comparison decision tree requires at least n! leaves.
A binary tree with height h has at most 2^h leaves; therefore h is at least ceil(log2(n!)).
Since n! is at least (n/2)^(n/2) up to harmless integer rounding, log2(n!) is Omega(n log n). Thus every exact sequential comparison sorter has worst-case comparison complexity Omega(n log n).
The supplied O(n log n) algorithm matches this lower bound, yielding Theta(n log n) comparison complexity.
derivations.result: c1 is established: O(n log n) is asymptotically optimal under the frozen comparison model.
residual_mismatch: The conclusion does not cover non-comparison algorithms, preprocessing, approximate sorting, or alternative parallel or computational models.

synthesis.selected_option: O(n log n) exact sequential comparison sort
basis: The decision-tree lower bound is Omega(n log n).
The supplied algorithmic upper bound is O(n log n).
conditions: The frozen model and exactness requirements remain unchanged.
